name: zod-validation-patterns
description: This skill provides comprehensive patterns for using Zod validation library in TypeScript applications. It ensures input validation is done correctly, securely, and consistently across the codebase.
capabilities: Create type-safe validation schemas for all data types including strings, numbers, dates, objects, arrays, and unions
Define custom validation logic using refinements for complex business rules like cross-field validation
Transform and normalize data during validation with built-in type coercion and preprocessing
Implement async validation for database checks and external API verification
Generate TypeScript types automatically from Zod schemas using z.infer
Handle validation errors with user-friendly messages and internationalization support
author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc
securitySummary: This skill is a documentation resource for the Zod validation library. All 970 static findings are false positives. The analyzer misidentified TypeScript code examples (using backticks in markdown) as Ruby shell commands. No actual executable code, cryptographic operations, or credential handling exists in this skill. All network references are legitimate documentation URLs to zod.dev, typescriptlang.org, and nextjs.org. This is a safe, read-only documentation skill for input validation patterns.
valueStatement: TypeScript applications often lack proper input validation, leading to runtime errors and security vulnerabilities. This skill provides comprehensive Zod validation patterns to validate API requests, form data, and user inputs with type-safe schemas.
limitations: This skill provides patterns and examples; it does not execute validation at runtime
Schema definitions must be written in TypeScript; this skill does not generate code automatically
Performance characteristics vary; validation overhead should be considered for high-throughput paths
Async validation patterns require proper error handling and timeout management

useCases.targetUser: Backend developers building web APIs with Next.js
useCases.title: Validate API request bodies in Next.js routes
useCases.description: Use Zod schemas to validate incoming JSON request bodies in Next.js API routes and Server Actions, returning clear error responses for invalid inputs.

useCases.title: Validate form submissions with type-safe schemas
useCases.description: Define schemas for form fields including email, password, phone numbers, and custom validators to ensure data integrity before processing.

useCases.title: Create reusable validation patterns for common data types
useCases.description: Build a library of reusable schemas for emails, passwords, URLs, UUIDs, and other common data types to ensure consistency across the codebase.

promptTemplates.title: Create basic validation schema
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need to validate structured data like API requests or form submissions

promptTemplates.title: Add custom validation rules
promptTemplates.scenario: When built-in validators are not enough and you need complex business logic validation

promptTemplates.title: Transform data during validation
promptTemplates.prompt:
Create a Zod schema that transforms input data:
1. Trim whitespace from strings
2. Convert strings to lowercase for emails
3. Coerce numbers from string inputs
4. Set default values for missing optional fields

Show the complete schema with transforms and demonstrate the transformation behavior.
promptTemplates.scenario: When you need to normalize or clean data as part of validation

promptTemplates.title: Implement async database validation
promptTemplates.prompt:
Create an async Zod validator that checks:
- Username uniqueness by querying a database
- Email format and domain restrictions
- Required async permission checks

Use refine with async functions and show proper error handling with safeParse.
promptTemplates.scenario: When validation requires external data sources like databases or APIs

bestPractices: Use safeParse instead of parse to avoid exceptions and handle errors gracefully in production code
Validate at system boundaries like API routes, Server Actions, and external data sources
Define custom error messages for user-facing validation to improve user experience
Reuse common validation schemas across your codebase to ensure consistency
antiPatterns: Using parse without try-catch in production code can cause unhandled exceptions
Re-validating already validated internal data adds unnecessary overhead
Missing error paths in refinements makes debugging validation failures difficult
Hardcoding validation logic in components instead of reusable schemas

faq.question: What is Zod and why should I use it?
faq.answer: Zod is a TypeScript-first validation library that creates schemas to validate data at runtime. Unlike TypeScript types which only exist at compile time, Zod schemas can validate actual data at runtime while automatically inferring TypeScript types from your schemas.

faq.question: How does Zod compare to other validation libraries?
faq.answer: Zod is known for its developer experience with a chainable API, strong TypeScript inference, and minimal bundle size. It is smaller than Joi but offers similar functionality with better TypeScript integration.

faq.question: Can Zod validate async data?
faq.answer: Yes, Zod supports async validation using the refine and superRefine methods with async functions. This enables database uniqueness checks, API calls, and other async operations as part of validation.

faq.question: How do I handle validation errors in user interfaces?
faq.answer: Use safeParse to get a result object with success/failure state. On failure, use result.error.format() to get a formatted error object that maps field paths to error messages, which is ideal for displaying in forms.

faq.question: What types can Zod validate?
faq.answer: Zod validates all JavaScript types including primitives (string, number, boolean, date), objects, arrays, maps, sets, unions, tuples, enums, literals, branded types, recursive schemas, and more.

faq.question: How do I create a schema from an existing TypeScript type?
faq.answer: For simple types, use z.infer<typeof schema> to extract the TypeScript type. To validate against an existing type, you would typically define the schema first and then use z.infer to get the type, rather than creating a schema from a type.
